item.title: Feature: Nintendo Life eShop Selects (May 2026)
item.content:encoded:
And we're back for the latest round of eShop Selects, looking back at last month's eShop-only releases and picking our top three favourites.

Plenty of games came out in May, although many of them also got day-and-date physical releases, so we only had six games that met our threshold this month (including one that's only on mobiles). Let's hop to it, shall we?

Here are the rules: Our writers and contributors vote for their top three eShop games we reviewed (and scored at least a 7/10) that launched in May 2026 on the eShop for Switch 1 and/or Switch 2. We then tally the votes and the three games with the highest number of votes take to the podium.

Honourable Mentions

One digital-only game that may have qualified for this is Kabuto Park, a cute little bug catcher we're in the process of reviewing, so keep an eye out for our verdict next week.

Behind the obvious front-runner, it was a tight contest this month. But before we get to the top three, here are the three that just missed out (including a mobile game that's not technically on the eShop, but Nintendo made it):

  • Coffee Talk Tokyo (NS1/2) | Review: 8/10
  • TetherGeist (NS1) | Review: 8/10
  • Pictonico! (Mobile) | Review: 8/10

3rd Place - Sektori (Switch 2)

Taking bronze is the addictive, Geometry Wars-inspired Sektori, which landed on Switch 2 in the middle of May. Ollie gave it a sparkling 9/10 in his review, calling it his "favourite Switch 2 release of 2026 so far."

Beyond its beautiful, score-chasing, twin-stick shooting, it was heartening to hear that developer Kimmo Lahtinen found success with the port, with sales of the Switch 2 version recouping "a living salary" for the solo dev after years of investment and overheads.

2nd Place - Mixtape (Switch 2)

Next up is Beethoven and Dinosaur's Mixtape, a low-stakes teen journey filled with licensed music, inventive visuals, and '80s/'90s nostalgia.

Putting all the embarrassing online 'discourse' it provoked to the side, it's undoubtedly a Marmite game that relies on your enjoyment and/or memories of the era it depicts (and you'll probably want to steer clear if you're irritated by pretentious teens and coming-of-age drama). But we loved Mixtape.

"This is a thoughtfully curated collection of music, sure," said Roland in his 9/10 review, "but before that, it’s an exciting, sentimental, funny game."

1st Place - Mina the Hollower (Switch 1 & 2)

And #1 by a wide margin is Yacht Club's Mina The Hollower. This was delayed out of last year, and we're so happy that it turned out to be worth the wait.

This dark, top-down affair may look like a Game Boy Color-ful, Zelda-style adventure, but the Shovel Knight devs refuse to hold your hand, and its openness and willingness to leave you to your own devices is a huge part of its charm.

"Mina the Hollower, like Shovel Knight before it, pays careful homage to a beloved genre and visual style, while also blending in modern design sensibilities," said Ken in his 9/10 review. We'll surely be discussing this more come GOTY time.

< Nintendo eShop Selects - April 2026

Do you agree with our ranking? Tell us about your favourite eShop releases of last month by voting in our poll and sharing your thoughts in the comments.

How we decide our eShop Selects top three: As we reach the end of every month, the Nintendo Life staff vote on their favourite titles from a list of games selected by the editorial team. To qualify for this list, these games must have been released as a digital-only Nintendo Switch eShop title in that particular month, and must have been reviewed on Nintendo Life; we select the qualifying games based on their review scores.

Staff are then asked to vote for three games that they think deserve to sit right at the very top of that list; first choice gets three points, second choice gets two points, and third choice gets one point. These votes are then tallied to create a top-three list, with the overall winner taking that month's top prize.



https://www.nintendolife.com/features/nintendo-life-eshop-selects-may-2026

item.title: Ro Laren Stars In Star Trek Horror Game From Silent Hill Remake Makers
item.content:encoded:
While the fate of Isaac Clark is still up in the air, space-based survival gaming has an unexpected hero in orbit. A familiar one at that. Unable to go more than 48 hours without announcing something new, Polish horror studio Bloober Team unveiled a game set in the Star Trek universe starring a fan-favorite character.

In the brief teaser for Star Trek: Shadow Frontier, we witness a derelict spacecraft cluttered with debris, concerning growths and startling creatures. A pre-recorded audio log reveals our protagonist to be Ro Laren, the hot headed Bajoran whose rankles with authority gave way to devotion to Starfleet. Given that there’s more grease on her face than her hair, Shadow Frontier is likely set around her recent Picard appearances. The Steam page says that Laren has arrived at a planet besieged by an omnipotent entity, something that the Star Trek universe, from false Vulcan gods to the Continuum, is rife with.

It has been a very busy Summer Games Fest for ol’ Bloober. Throughout the week they unveiled DLC for Cronos: The New Dawn and their adaptation of SAW, Genesis, a multiplayer game set around World War 1. This is on top of a new Layers of Fear, expanding on their first hit series.

While tie-ins like Blair Witch have brought out the worst in Bloober, the more troubling area is the launch of Paramount Games Studio. The new AAA gaming initiative by David Ellison, whose management has run amok, made itself known this week with The Last Ronin, a title from PlatinumGames based on the gritty Ninja Turtles comic. Ellison’s ambitions of purchasing and merging with Warner Bros. would further rock the industry, as that would include their portfolio of gaming works, from Batman to Mortal Kombat.

That said there seems to be an inverse relationship between the logic of a Star Trek game pitch and the execution. Sure, a Star Trek survival horror seems out of pocket, but in February Daedalic Entertainment released Across the Unknown, an RPG based around the early episodes of Star Trek: Voyager. Despite it all, it rocks.



https://kotaku.com/ro-laren-stars-in-star-trek-horror-game-from-silent-hill-remake-makers-2000703593
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Star Wars Zero Company, the XCOM-like strategy game, gets a release date
item.content:encoded:
EA has dropped a cinematic teaser for its upcoming Star Wars game, Star Wars Zero Company, and confirmed a release date: 27th August, 2026.

In a three-minute teaser shared during the Summer Game Fest activities, it gives a closer look at the mechanics and gameplay, including its XCOM-like permadeath feature, where "every strike counts, every move counts, and every choice counts".

If you don't mind a couple of light spoilers about who you'll be meeting during your adventure, there's also a lovely reveal at the end, too – you can check it out below:

"Every Move Counts. Command an elite squad through a gritty and authentic story in Star Wars Zero Company, a single-player turn-based tactics game launching on PC, PlayStation 5, and Xbox Series X|S," the video blurb teases. "Coming August 27, 2026.

"Each choice you make could alter the fate of the galaxy in this cinematic original story set in the twilight of the Clone Wars Era."

Pre-order ahead of August's street date, and you'll secure the Crystallinbe Astromech Comsmetic Pack.

As Fran summarised for us recently, the original story – led by Jedi: Fallen Order and Survivor veteran, Aaron Contreras – is set during the Clone Wars. Clankers and Separatists of various kinds are among the foes you'll be facing, but things become more interesting when a mysterious Dark Side cult comes in, empowering regular people using evil ghosts.

As Fallen Order and Survivor have already leaned on the more surprising side of Star Wars' mythology, it appears Zero Company may be doubling down on such ideas while retaining "the grittier style of Rogue One" and the prestige TV prequel Andor.

Mentioned in this article



https://www.eurogamer.net/star-wars-zero-company-release-date

item.title: Blood Dungeon is a Refreshing Spin on the Survivors Formula
item.content:encoded:
I love to throw on a podcast or long-winded YouTube video on one screen while doing a run or five on Vampire Survivors. Well, I've gone and completed that game 100 percent, but I think I just found a worthy replacement.

Blood Dungeon is the latest title from developer Messhof, most known for creating the Nidhogg series. Blood Dungeon is familiar for fans of Survivors-like, but it throws in a few twists that makes it feel fresh and different.

During Summer Game Fest Play Days, I had the pleasure to check this title out. The good news is, there's a public demo available, so if you like what you hear, definitely give it a go. As a bonus, I played it on Steam Deck and it worked spectacularly.

For a game of this genre, you still have your standard auto-attacking that you'd expect, but Blood Dungeon is also a platformer. For as much as I love Vampire Survivors, it's very one-dimensional.

Here, on the other hand, we have a whole new layer to contend with as far as movement is concerned. Your character can climb walls, either hanging on the side or from above. Hang from steel grates, or monkey your way across chains. Avoid damage by any means, while dishing it out with increasingly powerful attacks.

Enemy waves gradually ramp up in intensity. Stronger enemies, more enemies, they'll be thrown in your path. Constant movement is key, while also collecting the blood of fallen enemies to level up.

Level-ups will grant you upgrades to your weapons, which range anywhere from pistols and machine guns to noxious gas clouds and spider webs.

The spider web weapon is one I want to pay special emphasis to, as this creates more obstacles to grasp to in the environment, better enabling you to avoid damage. Likewise, I found a jetpack in another run which allowed me to hover around.

Various characters are unlockable and each one has some uniqueness to them, with each level-up granting a percent increase to cooldown and the like. There's one character that is just a fly, and well, it flies around. It sort of delegitimizes the need for platforming, though each character has their own benefit.

All of this gameplay is under the guise of an intentionally sketchy, sort of rugged look. It's the visual style you'd associate with a developer like Messhof, unafraid of being a bit different, goofy and weird.

This demo version of Blood Dungeon fast-tracked the progress with a bunch of already-unlocked upgrades. Metaprogression like increases to stats are present, so that'll keep players coming back as the gameplay gradually becomes meatier and meatier after each session.

There's also various biomes, all with their own twist. Water with an oxygen gauge was something I had to contend with, for instance. There's also a daily run that refreshes every 24 hours, so that will definitely give me an incentive to come back and play Blood Dungeon again.

Blood Dungeon was previewed during Summer Game Fest 2026 Play Days.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/blood-dungeon-is-refreshing-spin-on-survivors-formula

item.title: Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2 Draws You Into An Action Packed Alien World With Fun Loadouts And Satisfying Combat
item.content:encoded:
One of the multiple Alien games that have made their appearance across Summer Game Fest is Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2. Leaning heavily into the back-half of an Alien film in this game, you and up to three friends will have boots on the ground to try to investigate a strange situation that evolves into a nightmare.

I sat down to play a mission about 1/3rd of the way through the game. Armed as a Specialist, the class focused on customization. We dropped into a mission where our team had noticed a ship that had gone dark on the far side of the planet. Dropping down, it wasn't long before the fireteam was getting swarmed by rogue synths and a whole bevy of Xenomorphs.

If you want to learn more about what has gone into creating the game, difficulty options, and how it was to work with 20th Century Fox check out our interview with Chris Cross, Studio Creative/Design Director at Cold Iron Studios

In terms of tone there definitely isn't the level of stress and tension that you might expect from the 'horror' style Alien games, instead Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2 embodies that final act of an alien film where the monster has been seen and is no longer in the shadows, and you're ready to do everything you can to survive mowing your way through a large number of foes.

Before getting into the gameplay, we'll need to explain the classes a bit.

Loadout And Controls

Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2 has six different classes that you can play as: the Duelist, Machinist, Marauder, Hunter, and Medic. There is an additional sixth class that I got to play as, the Specialist. This class is the jack of all trades that doesn't just have its own unique abilities, but is able to equip any of the abilities from the other classes.

Interestingly, the Specialist gets access to the abilities of the other classes once you've leveled up those classes enough. In this way, especially if you want to take on some of the more challenging difficulties, you'll want to practice with each of the classes, understand their mechanics and unlock their abilities, and then build a Specialist that caters very specifically to your playstyle.

During my time with the game, I was playing a Specialist who had access to an assault rifle, flamethrower, a powerful explosive rocket, and a grenade for lobbing.

Diving into the level, the action kicked off pretty much immediately as synths flooded into a large control room. I was a big fan of the unsettling blank expressions on the synth's faces and how some would swiftly jump over barriers to try to advance, while others almost tumbled.

They were almost zombie-like in their approach.

My squad, which comprised one of the developers and two AI companions, began to immediately mow down all of the enemies that came our way. As they poured in from different sides of the rooms in waves, there was a fun sense of covering one another's backs as you stopped seeing enemies and started hunting them out in the room.

As we dove deeper into the dilapidated facility, the waves of enemy synths were joined by xenomorphs crawling across the floors and ceilings, and exploding out of vents in the walls.

Variation in enemy design and their weaknesses

In combat, the Xenomorphs came in a variety of different varieties. The standard Xenomorph was human-sized and would crawl its way towards you; on top of that, there was a far larger variant that would hide in vents and pop out. There was also a variant that contained poison that you could spot with glowing spores on its head that, upon death, would pop, creating a hazard where it stood for some time.

Synths, too, had varieties, though these were pulling from the most commonly seen humanoid enemy variations. Some would approach with guns, some would have large shields, and there were even Synths that, after defeat, would trigger a bomb in their chest to explode, trying to take out any nearby Marines.

You can get through combat encounters with any weapons, but for the sake of conserving ammunition, there are also a variety of weapon types that can be more effective on certain enemies. When the Xenos started swarming, the flamethrower would make quick work of them, a downside of that being that your view would generally be crowded by fire. The Synths were especially weak to the Cryo weapons. Picking up a Cryo grenade, I was able to pitch it into a group of Synths and then shoot them, watching them shatter and hit the ground in a satisfying shower of parts.

The gun handling was incredibly satisfying. Short bursts from the assault rifle left Sync heads exploding or having them stumble from damaged limbs. 

There was such a stark and recognizable difference between the eerie pale light of the Synth, easy to see but coming at you like an overflowing wave, and the black carapace of the Aliens swarming towards you. Even if they weren't too difficult to kill at the difficulty level we were playing on, there was still a very real feeling that the swarm could overcome you.

Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2 feels like a fast-paced final act to an Alien film

While only getting to play a short portion from the middle of Aliens: Fireteam Elite 2, I was incredible impressed with the world that had been built for the game, how satisfying the combat was along with the loadout crafting, and how much care had gone into ensuring that it would be a fun game to play with friends but also one you would have the satisfaction of playing again at new difficulties or with added challenges.

I can definitely see myself getting a few friends together in a voice chat to chat and joke our way through an adventure like this.

Aliens: FIreteam Elite 2 was previewed at Summer Game Fest 2026 over the course of 30 minutes.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/aliens-fireteam-elite-2-draws-you-into-action-packed-alien-world-with-fun-loadouts

item.title: Expeditions: Samurai Drops You Into Sengoku Japan as a Privateer, Not William Adams — and That Choice Reshapes the Story
item.content:encoded:
Yesterday, publisher THQ Nordic announced Expeditions: Samurai, the next game in the Expeditions RPG series. It is being developed by Campfire Cabal, a Copenhagen-based studio founded in 2022 and made up largely of former Logic Artists developers after the studio was closed down.

In a media presentation attended by Wccftech, the team said it sees itself as the creator of the Expeditions IP, which has sold just over 1.3 million copies across the series. They presented the game after surviving a difficult studio period in 2023, when the team was nearly shut down and later rebuilt with support from THQ Nordic.

For those unfamiliar with the series, Expeditions is a saga of historical alternative-history RPGs: grounded in real people, real places, and real events, but shaped by player choice so history can unfold differently. The team says its audience skews older, around 20+, and that its games prioritize strong storytelling, tactical choices, and long-term consequences.

Setting and Premise

As suggested by its title, Expeditions: Samurai is set in feudal Japan during the Sengoku period, the age of warring states and constant civil conflict. The game begins shortly before the Battle of Sekigahara, which is treated as the turning point that ends the Sengoku era and marks the beginning of a new era of peace. It is also set about 50 years after the first Europeans arrived in Japan, meaning the protagonist is not the first foreigner the Japanese have seen, but he is the first Protestant, which the team says matters to the story.

The story is set in 1600, when the navigator William Adams arrived in Japan and later became the first European to be granted the title of “Samurai.” In Expeditions: Samurai, however, you do not play as Adams himself; instead, you arrive in Japan first, leading a crew of privateers aboard the Dutch frigate De Albatros. From there, you are drawn into the civil war nearing its climax, navigating the politics, intrigue, and drama of feudal Japan as your crew comes into contact with powerful warlords whose struggle will shape the nation’s future.

The team says the setting was chosen partly because fans overwhelmingly wanted Japan. Rather than make a fantasy Japan game, they wanted to stay grounded in the historical reality of the Sengoku period and tell a fictionalized story inside that framework. That means the game is still an Expeditions title, but with a distinctly Japanese historical focus.

The developers claimed they wanted to shift the art direction slightly compared with earlier entries. Their goal was to keep the game historically authentic while bringing back more personality and expression, closer to the feel of the older games than the more realistic direction of Expeditions: Rome. They describe the result as detailed realism: grounded in reality, but with stronger character and expression.

The game is built in Unreal Engine 5, and they want that technology to be visible in the final presentation. So the look is not meant to be fantasy stylization; it is meant to feel real, but still vivid and characterful.

Core Gameplay

The Expeditions series is built around a hybrid of real-time exploration and turn-based combat. According to Campfire Cabal, combat is meant to be cerebral rather than dependent on luck, with success driven mostly by tactical understanding rather than dice rolls. Character classes are a major feature, and replayability is an important part of the formula because different classes can significantly change how the game unfolds.

The series also emphasizes choice and consequence. Players can pursue violence or diplomacy, and now, for the first time in the series, stealth is presented as a major third pillar for resolving obstacles and a full alternative to combat. The new dynamic combat initiation system lets players sneak, split the party, position characters, and launch surprise attacks from stealth. They can also use stealth outside combat to manipulate enemies with distractions, smoke bombs, and other tools. The team says players may even be able to avoid combat entirely in many situations if they master stealth. The enemy AI underneath this system was built by veterans of the Hitman series, which they cite as the reason the stealth layer feels so robust.

They also stressed deep character management, including resources, injuries, weapons, armor, and keeping party members alive. In the game, players can become a samurai lord or lady of a village in service to Tokugawa Ieyasu. The game includes village-management elements, so the player is not only fighting and exploring but also helping shape a local power base. They also say the player will participate in major historical events, including the Battle of Sekigahara, depending on choices. The game will let players roam a large world map and tackle quests in whatever order they prefer.

Companions

Expeditions: Samurai features eight companion characters, each with their own quest line. The developers put much more effort into companions this time because previous Expeditions games did not always have the resources to expand those relationships as much as they wanted. These companions can become friends, enemies, or romance options, and every companion is romanceable. The team presented this as a major part of the game's emotional core, and they expect players to grow very attached to these characters throughout the campaign.

Co-op, Controls, and Accessibility

One of the biggest new features of this installment is the inclusion of a full two-player campaign co-op. This is the first time the series has had true co-op, replacing the old makeshift hot-seat style from earlier games. Co-op is built around simultaneous combat resolution, meaning both players can move, plan, and set up actions at the same time before ending their turns.

Dialogue is also shared in co-op. One player can speak while the other observes, and the players can swap who controls the conversation. The game also uses both characters’ skills in dialogue checks, so co-op can open additional options. The system supports drop-in, drop-out play, so a campaign can shift between solo and co-op without breaking progression.

Expeditions: Samurai has full controller support built in from the ground up. This was not true to the same extent in the previous franchise installment, 2022's Expeditions: Rome, but this time it is a core part of the design. They specifically mention support for DualSense, Xbox controllers, handheld devices, and custom controllers.

The game will include difficulty settings with customizable tuning for different systems, such as combat and resource management. The developers wish to accommodate different player preferences without flattening the tactical depth.

Early Access and Release Plan

The game is planned to launch in Early Access in August. The Early Access version will include Act One, which the team estimates at around 8 hours of quests and says will be almost feature-complete. After that, there will be three major updates before 1.0, including two gameplay updates and one story update that unlocks Act Two.

The full 1.0 release is planned for early 2027. At launch, the game is expected to have four main story acts and about 60 hours of total playtime. The team also expects around 45+ levels, 100+ events and encounters, and a large explorable world map.

PC is the primary platform for both Early Access and full release. They are testing on the Steam Deck and ensuring the game works well on handheld devices. Console plans are not locked down, but they say a console release may happen later, depending on how the game performs. They specifically mentioned that they are not currently promising Switch 2, only that it could be considered later.

As a side note, when asked directly, the team said no generative AI was used in the creation of Expeditions: Samurai. They said everything shown in the game was made by a human being and that they are not fans of generative AI.

About the author: With over two decades of experience in gaming journalism, Alessio Palumbo has led the gaming vertical at Wccftech since August 2015. He started working at a young age for Italian websites like Everyeye.it, Gamestar.it, Nextgame.it, and Multiplayer.it before kickstarting the indie English-language publication Worlds Factory as its founder and Editor in Chief. In the last decade, he has coordinated the overall output of Wccftech's gaming section, managed PR relations, assigned reviews, produced daily news coverage, edited gaming content as needed, and delivered game reviews. Arguably, his trademark content is the long series of exclusive developer interviews that have been cited by Wikipedia and by the biggest news media and gaming publications. His passion for technology also makes him knowledgeable when it comes to gaming hardware and tech. His favorite genres include RPGs, MMORPGs, and action/adventure games.

Follow Wccftech on Google to get more of our news coverage in your feeds.



https://wccftech.com/expeditions-samurai-sengoku-japan-dutch-privateer/

item.title: Escape Academy 2 Is Co-Op Puzzle Perfection
item.content:encoded:
Every once in a while you have the fortune of running across a game that just feels made for you. Myself and colleague Andrew Stretch had a chance to check out Escape Academy 2, which showed that escape rooms in an open world is a match made in heaven.

If you want to learn even more about Escape Academy 2, check out our interview with Mike Salyh, Co-Founder of Coin Crew Games.

The very essence of escape room design is exploration and environmental storytelling, which Escape Academy 2 embraces fully. There is something to see just about everywhere you look and a reward for creatively checking around every corner.

That rewarding exploration bears out in the world and lore of Escape Academy 2 as well. Where the story and lore played a small role in the first, developer Coin Crew Games wanted the characters and academy to have equal footing with the gameplay this time around.

The academy is full of people, from the wacky professors to other students around campus. The professors are the real drivers of the story and larger than life, but there are a ton of students with side quests/puzzles that range from something as simple as get me a bag of chips to figuring out some elaborate puzzle they themselves have devised. 

Our short time made us feel like we were apart of the academy as well. In one quest, we had to collect random items like a toy boat and train for a professor. Those items then showed up in the following escape room puzzle we had to complete.

Where Escape Academy 2 really shines is in its co-op experience. I am certain I would have enjoyed my time with the game if I had played it by myself, but it is such an amazing co-op experience that it really feels like the most ideal way to play.

And I don't mean that in the sense that two brains are better than one, but that the simple ways in which you can more easily work with someone is a joy. For example, your partner looking at a list of symbols and relaying information while you're inputting them into keypad to unlock a door.

It's so natural to just fall into a rhythm and communicate what you see and are doing, which is oddly rare with co-op games. Too often, games are about just simply existing in the same space where Escape Academy 2 seamlessly lets you work collaboratively. 

The puzzle design we were able to interact with was just delightful as well. There is a logic to everything, and everyday objects work just how you would think. Interacting with any object felt natural and realistic in the sense that I was never caught off guard by what I anticipated a certain object to do.

On top of that, some aspects of a puzzle are not signposted to you at all but are part of the natural environment. It's easy to see that the doors with weird symbols above them are a puzzle, but maybe not so much with a clipboard with some writing on it.

One puzzle had a solution hidden in just that, a normal clipboard with the text of a report on a failed science experiment. Reading it gave us the information we needed to read a chart that then corresponded with a combination lock on it.

We ended our session with a more advanced escape room that had a 30-minute timer attached to it for completion. There were a ton of interlocking puzzles that were all connected by some crazy Rube Goldberg machine whose goal was to get a rat through a series of tubes so it'd finally reach a wheel to power a light.

Within that room, which was split in two, there were more than ten unique puzzles, all of which were very different from one another, to solve. Some were connected, needing to solve one to then get access to the next, some were more standalone, but all worked toward the goal.

For example, one really cool solution was tied to some colored paper airplanes you could launch in the air. Each only traveled a certain distance, and the order of those colored planes from closest to furthest was a key component to finding out how to unlock something.

There are a ton of examples I could go into, but suffice to say Escape Academy 2 is great at giving you triumphant moment after triumphant moment as you solve a particular puzzle or unlock a door. The next serotonin dump is always just around the corner.

Escape Academy 2 Preview | Final Thoughts

In total, Escape Academy 2 is aiming to be about a 15-hour game with a ton of puzzles, a mystery about the academy itself, and a lot of charm in its characters. The open world of the academy grounds is great, and the moment-to-moment gameplay of puzzling was wonderful.

Escape Academy 2 is joining the crowded month of September for releases, but I can tell you that myself and Andrew Stretch will be keeping a close eye on this. It's a no-brainer for puzzle fans, particularly if you liked things like Blue Prince or Animal Well.

Escape Academy 2 was previewed at Summer Game Fest Play Days.

Andrew is the Editor in Chief at TechRaptor. Conned into a love of gaming by Nintendo at a young age, Andrew has been chasing the dragon spawned by… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/escape-academy-2-preview-co-op-puzzle-perfection
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Escape Academy 2 Emphasizes The Academy With School-Wide Open World Puzzles - Interview
item.content:encoded:
At Summer Game Fest, Coin Crew Games was ready to show off the latest build of Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School. After getting to experience some of the open world gameplay of the academy, complete some of the unique side-puzzles, and help a rat make its way through a series of puzzles to power a lightbulb, we got to sit with Mike Salyh, Co-Founder of Coin Crew Games, to discuss puzzle design, how the sequel has grown, and how they're making sure that anyone can play Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School.

If you want to learn more about our time with Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School you can check out Andrew Otton's preview.

In the initial Escape Academy, you would move from escape room to escape room, selecting them on the map. While the landscape and structure of the academy were present, it was simply a map to cursor through. Having had a chance to walk, interact, and even platform through the open world, I was interested to know what had given Coin Crew games the push to expand the world so much.

To understand the proper scale of Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School, Salyh told us that while the first game should take a player about 4 hours to complete, the sequel should take players 15 hours.

More Academy to complement the Escape

"When we made the first Escape Academy, we really liked the world we had built for the game," Salyh explained, "All of the characters and puzzle humor. Let's say the first game was 90% Escape and 10% academy, so when we wanted to make a sequel, we wanted to get closer to 50% escape and 50% academy."

From our exploration of the open world, we didn't just travel from location to location but ran into other students looking for our help that required a puzzle to be solved to proceed, hidden environmental elements, and more. At one point, while exploring, I had ended up platforming up to the top of a building, and even higher to the top of some stove pipe towers.

"I wanted to give you more opportunities to run around the school, talk to other students, and find secrets. This lent itself to this open-world style of gameplay."

There's been a big trend for puzzle games recently to push players to solve puzzles outside of the game. When asking for the range of puzzles from the first Escape Academy to recent meta-puzzlers like Animal Well, Blue Prince, or Tunic, Salyh first shared his own love of those titles before explaining where Escape Academy 2 falls on the spectrum.

"I really love all of the games on that list. With this game, we were focused a bit more on 'Escape Rooms'. There are definitely some secrets that span across the entire school, and there's at least one puzzle we put in there where we were like 'this is an Easter Egg of an Easter Egg.'"

Escape Academy 2 Is Making Sure All Players Can Complete The Game

The puzzles that we completed relied on us to listen to chirping birds, understand links between paper airplanes and symbols, and watch light cast from a prism. I was interested to hear how puzzle design was implemented for those with limited hearing or color blindness.

"This is one of my favorite parts of the process, we have a Discord where people can sign up to playtest, and so getting a lot of players to play through it's been insightful. We have a colorblind mode in the game, where, when you turn it on, for any color-specific puzzle, it will include a label saying what color it is."

Continuing to explain some of the accessibility options, Salyh said: "We have a similar thing for audio-specific puzzles where you can turn on closed captions."

One of the puzzles that we completed required us to listen carefully, but due to the busy nature of Summer Game Fest, it was hard to hear exactly what was going on but we did notice a hint to our solution in the captions. What allowed for the solution of the puzzle, about correctly describing a bird who wakes early, I was able to solve based on my knowledge of birds.

On hearing that Salyh was happy to hear, he explained that they always wanted to make sure that if a player had knowledge about a certain subject, it could help them solve puzzles. "If it's a subject you know, we want to reward you. We don't try to trust outside knowledge, but if you've got it, then it should count and help you."

Something that I definitely had to compliment Escape Academy 2 on was its approach to split-screen, treating both players as separate entities. It was really refreshing in a co-op scenario that when the other player is interacting with a character for a quest or going through a puzzle, the other player is left to freely roam.

On top of that, if the other party member happens to be in conversation with an NPC, you can see the speech bubbles of their conversation.

Escape Academy 2: Back 2 School will be released in September 2026.

Andrew has written Video Game and Entertainment news, reviews, and guides for 10+ years. As Senior Content Manager, he assists in creating and editing… More about Andrew



https://techraptor.net/gaming/features/escape-academy-2-emphasizes-academy-with-school-wide-open-world-puzzles-interview

item.title: Gears E-Day and Halo Campaign Evolved Preorders To Go Live Today
item.content:encoded:
A reliable insider has confirmed that players can preorder Gears of War E-Day and Halo Campaign Evolved later today.

The XBOX Games Showcase and Gears of War E-Day Direct is all set to go live later tonight, and there are two games that players are sure to be a part of the event: Halo: Campaign Evolved and Gears E-Day. Now, players also know that tonight is the night when they receive release dates for both.

Gears E-Day and Halo Campaign Evolved Preorder and Price Details Revealed

The information regarding Gears E-Day and Halo Campaign Evolved preorders comes through a report by billbil-kun on Dealabs. The report states that preorders will begin right after tonight’s showcase for both titles. At the moment, there is some confusion about the PS5 version of Gears of War E-Day; however, the Xbox Series X/S and PC versions can be preordered later tonight for $69.99.

For Halo Campaign Evolved, on the other hand, a PS5 version is planned, but the report can’t confirm whether it will follow the same schedule as the other platforms. The price for Halo Campaign Evolved has been set at $49.99 USD and €59.99 EUR for all platforms. The release dates for both games weren’t revealed in the latest report; however, the release date for Halo Campaign Evolved surfaced a few months ago.

In other news, new information and images have begun to leak concerning Halo: Campaign Evolved’s preorder bonuses. Additionally, a new report covers details on the next mainline campaign project from Halo Studios and includes a job listing. What are your thoughts on Gears E-Day and Halo Campaign Evolved Preorders going live today? Leave your thoughts down in the comments, and join the official Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more information from Insider Gaming, read about the Little Nightmares 3 ‘The Backstage’ DLC. Don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: Blood Message is a Brutal, Cinematic Experience
item.content:encoded:
Summer Game Fest 2026 was a resounding success, with plenty of exciting trailers from beloved IPs and developers. The one that blew me away the most, however, was most unexpected: Blood Message.

Set during the Tang Dynasty, the Golden Age of China, Blood Message offers an unrelenting, bloody, unglorified look at war and conflict. After getting my hands on it at Summer Game Fest's Play Days, I am convinced this is cooking up to be something special.

Developed by 24 Entertainment and published by NetEase, this is the first hands-on demo offered to media. I was eager to play this myself, since while the trailers thus far were incredible beautiful and cinematic, I really wanted to learn more about its gameplay.

But the thing that stood out to me first and foremost was the visuals, which is as beautiful as the trailers themselves. The setting of our gameplay session is in a settlement in the northwestern region of China. The terrain is rugged, sandy, and unapologetic.

A fitting location, then, for a game with combat just as brutal as the deserts it's set within. We begin by searching through a village. It's under attack by an unknown group of soldiers, and as I understand it, we're searching for our son with our brother.

Blood Message starts calm, but soon all hell breaks loose. Our brother gets in a tussle with a group of soldiers. I immediately rush to save him as other soldiers close in. Light attacks are fast and vicious, almost feral in a sense.

You're able to execute wounded enemies, slamming your cold steel on their necks. Again, it's a brutal and honest look at combat. Along with the light attacks are heavy attacks. What impressed me is the protagonist taking on a halfsword technique (essentially, grabbing the blade of the sword with one hand and the hilt with the other for extra weight and power).

These attacks slice through enemy shields and I mixed them with light attacks to take out my foes. There is a parry, which offers a generous window and staggers enemies. Once they're staggered, a light attack is a good option to finish them off.

Here's where Blood Message really shines, though. I haven't seen a game seamlessly blend cinematic and real-time, player-controlled combat quite like this. Enemies will grab you from behind and you'll have to mash a button to get out of their grip; meanwhile, swords will clash and sometimes you need to overpower your opponent in this struggle.

If you look at the trailer for Blood Message, you'll see that everything looks scripted. I was trying to interpret what the real gameplay was like, and as the trailer shows, it's actually quite accurate. You feel like you're in a cutscenes while throwing out these attacks.

When you think about swordplay in video games, it's actually quite a bit more sanitized than reality. Two humans are struggling to take each other's lives, and that's certainly reflected in the weight of our movement.

We also partake in several stealth segments, though if you are discovered you can just fight it out and hope to survive. Stealth kills are just as visceral, with the protagonist using a hammer to mercilessly end these soldiers.

Eventually, we're caught in a scripted sequence and have to escape. Soldiers are chasing us as arrows whizz by our head, and more foes are trying to intercept me on the way forward. Dashing through houses, crashing through scaffolding and jumping from rooftops, all I can do is run forward and keep going.

Our chase is interrupted by an enemy crashing into us from the left, pinning the protagonist against the wall. A brutal struggle ensues as I frantically tap square to stop him from plunging his dagger into my shoulder. It still stabs me despite the struggle, but I soon overpower the soldier and shove the dagger into his eye.

Overall, I get the sense that Blood Message is a linear experience, and perhaps even a shorter one. Even so, if it can maintain its strong momentum with such strong, compelling, cinematic scenes and combat encounters, I am all in.

The narrative, while hard to intuit from the demo, involves father and son, which is relatable even for those who aren't familiar with China's history. In that regard, I think it'll be easy to get invested, and it might even tug at my heart strings.

Blood Message was previewed at Summer Game Fest 2026 Play Days.

Austin cut his teeth writing various  fan-fiction stories on the RuneScape forums when he was in elementary school. Later on, he developed a deep love… More about Austin



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/blood-message-is-brutal-cinematic-experience

item.title: Kill some time with these much needed distractions
item.content:encoded:
Constantly being plugged into the news grind is mentally exhausting. Sometimes we just need to take a break, unwind, and do something fun. That’s why we’ve built up a collection of distracting time-wasters for when we need a break from being obsessively online. We figured you might enjoy these harmless rabbit holes, mildly addictive browser games, and internet curiosities, too, so we’ve been writing about them when we find them.

Can you beat our score in I’m Not a Robot or did you find a gem of an academic paper on motherhood and body horror on Horror Lex? Tell us in the comments.

The goal here isn’t to get engrossed in a game that you’ll lose hundreds of hours to, or become an expert on dialectical materialism. It’s to have a little fun on your lunch break, decompress between emails, or give you an interesting repository of art to dig through on a slow Sunday afternoon. So check back often to see the latest light-hearted (well, mostly light-hearted) time waster we’re passing around the office.

  • The Virtual OS Museum lets you relive over 600 operating systems right on your desktop

    The Virtual OS Museum isn’t a physical place, it’s a collection of over 1,700 distinct installations of over 600 operating systems for over 250 platforms that you can download and run via emulation right on your computer. It’s largely the work of one man, Andrew Warkentin, a developer and OS historian who has been slowly building his collection of OS images since 2003.

    The library spans nearly the entire history of computing from 1948’s Manchester Baby, the first stored computer program, to early builds of Android from 2011. Unsurprisingly, there are a lot of obscure OSes in there, including countless DOS variants, MOS for the Acorn BBC Master, and a number of hobby OSes like NitrOS-9, which brings a host of modern features to the ‘80s Tandy Radio Shack CoCo line.

    Read Article >

  • 82-0 is the best basketball game, to hell with NBA 2K

    82-0 marries the stat nerd fun of fantasy basketball with instant gratification and a bit of dumb luck. The goal is to draft a team of players that could (theoretically) have a perfect 82-0 season.

    Obviously, if you just had free rein to pick whoever you wanted from throughout history, there would be little challenge. The twist is that the site randomly selects a team and an era, and then you pick a single player. So, for example, if you pulled the Knicks and 2020s, you might pick Jalen Brunson as your point guard, then you’d get a new team and era to select your center from.

    Read Article >

  • Mechanical Pencil is the new cross-section book.

    If you grew up enjoying Stephen Biesty’s cross-sections like me, you’ll appreciate this brilliant project from Google engineer Bryan Macomber. The website breaks down mechanical objects, like the G2 retractable pen (my favorite), the PEZ dispenser, and the Zippo lighter. I can’t wait to see what comes next. (h/t to Kottke for spotting it.)

  • I’m beginning to wonder, am I a robot?

    I’m Not a Robot has a simple premise: Finish a series of CAPTCHA to prove your humanity. But quickly, your usual click-a-box or identify-the-stop-sign gives way to Where’s Waldo, Tic-Tac-Toe, and word searches. I got stuck at level 17, and now I’m not sure if I actually am a human.

  • New Art City is a free virtual gallery filled with beautifully bizarre art.

    There are dozens of exhibitions that you can walk through in your browser. Some feature 3D scanned sculptures, others are glitchy worlds populated by sound experiments, and some have branching poetry for you to explore. Each 3D world is “multiplayer”, so you can visit a virtual opening and chat with other art fans.

  • Horror Lex is a free database of academic literature for horror nerds.

    I love horror movies and overanalyzing things, so Horror Lex scratches a really specific itch. It doesn’t actually host any papers or books. Instead, it’s a hand-selected index of over 13,000 documents, many of them from peer-reviewed journals. A lot of them are free to read, too.

  • Spotify Wrapped? No. Welcome to Tax Wrapped.

    Okay: almost nobody wants a year-end “wrapped” for most things we do — food delivery, subscriptions, you name it. It’d be too embarrassing to look into that mirror. But this one seems legitimately useful to help Americans understand how their taxes are being spent. Enter Tax Wrapped, from Riley Walz. Find out how much of your money is going to health, war, and more.

  • I can’t stop playing this annoyingly hard color memory game.

    You get a few seconds to sear a color into your brain. Then you have to find it again with a set of hue, saturation, and brightness sliders. Then you do it four more times. You can challenge yourself, your friends, or play against the entire world.

  • Binary Piano is an addictive way to make algorithmic music in your browser.

    I love tools that let you make music based on a simple set of rules. Tim Holman created this one based on a YouTube video in which notes are triggered by a simple binary counter. Now if only I could get this out of Chrome and into my DAW.

  • Relive the glory days when MTV played music videos.

    MTV Rewind collects broadcast clips and music videos from YouTube and strings them together to recreate the experience of actually watching MTV in its heyday. There’s even a collection of 98 videos that attempts to piece together the first full day of broadcast, complete with classic bumpers.

  • A Swedish hot dog with shrimp salad is the most chaotic evil sandwich ever and I will hear no arguments to the contrary.

    The Sandwich Alignment Game is the time-waster you didn’t know you needed this weekend. You could lose serious time placing these 57 sandwiches on a D&D-style alignment grid. Now, some people might say this isn’t really a game, but I disagree. Deciding whether an eggplant parm or a meatball parm is more lawful than the other requires real mental dexterity.



https://www.theverge.com/entertainment/945205/weekend-time-waster-internet-browser-game-rabibit-hole

item.title: Gears of War: E-Day PS5 Version Debate Sparks Online
item.content:encoded:
There’s some confusion around Gears of War: E-Day’s PS5 version, with one insider saying it’s not coming and the other pointing to evidence of a physical release on the platform.

We are just hours away from today’s event, the Xbox Games Showcase, which will be followed directly by a Gears of War: E-Day Direct. By then, everything will be clarified. However, just a few hours before this event, some conflicting reports surfaced online regarding the PS5 release of the upcoming title.

Gears of War E-Day PS5 Release Was Planned, but Now Canceled

The first claim comes from Jeff Grubb on the Giant Bomb podcast (via Wario64), who stated that a PS5 version of Gears of War: E-Day existed but is no longer on the cards. During the episode, Grubb stated, “Gears of War: E-Day was gonna be on PS5. It’s not anymore. That just happened. That was a decision that just got made.” Grubb is great with XBOX-related stuff, so players will definitely believe him when he says the PS5 release is not happening anymore.

Gears of War E-Day PS5 Physical Edition Evidence and Rating Found

Then comes in billbil-kun from Dealabs, and they have some conflicting findings. Their report states that “the SKU for the PS5 physical version of Gears of War: E-Day does indeed exist”. It has also been assigned a UPC; however, Billbil-kun has also added that they are unsure whether the identifier will be retained or removed. Additionally, Gears of War: E-Day has been rated by PEGI for PS5, and the rating has appeared today across all platforms.

It’s difficult to confirm whether the PS5 release for Gears of War: E-Day is still coming or whether XBOX is going the exclusive route. Could it be a result of the PlayStation CEO’s decision to no longer release single-player games on PC? We don’t know, but all the confusion about Gears of War: E-Day’s PS5 release should be cleared later tonight.

In other news, a shameless Gears of War and Dead Space clone had appeared on the Nintendo eShop last month. What are your thoughts on Gears of War: E-Day’s PS5 release? Leave your thoughts down in the comments, and join the official Insider Gaming Discord server.

For more information from Insider Gaming, read about the Avatar: The Last Airbender RPG no longer moving forward. Don’t forget to sign up for our weekly newsletter.

item.title: Opinion: Mario Kart World Deserves Its Flowers, So Let's Stop Comparing It To MK8 Deluxe
item.content:encoded:
Sometimes, when I wind the window down in my car, I think about Mario Kart World. I’m sitting alone in the driver’s seat, radio playing some mid-2000s indie or alt-rock, a breeze blowing through my hair. It might be a quiet morning, the traffic light but the sun bright and warm. I could be admiring the scenery in a country I’m mostly used to, but still always curious to see what I spot. Or maybe I’m thinking about throwing a banana peel at the car ahead of me (I’m not, don’t do that).

I love Mario Kart World; it’s an oxymoron of a racer, the calmest and most chaotic experience you can have with friends or alone. It’s like going on a sightseeing road trip where, every so often, you’ll be thrust onto a motorway where cows and turtles and lightning bolts are being flung around everywhere.

But it also had the unenviable task of being the game to usher in the Switch 2 generation, the first brand new Mario Kart game in over a decade, and the follow-up to Mario Kart 8 Deluxe, arguably the Mario Kart game, bursting with almost 100 courses and 50 playable characters, if you include DLC. This shouldn’t be the standard for the series, because it’s unattainable. It’s the Smash Ultimate of Mario Kart.

So I wish people would stop comparing World to 8 Deluxe. The latter is a celebration of all things Mario Kart and a complete blow-out for the series: it’s a complete anomaly, too, being not just the best-selling Mario Kart game of all time, but the best-selling Mario game ever. It’s also a pretty tame sequel: yes, there’s an abundance of content, but what’s really new about MK8? Anti-gravity? Four new items?

Because at the core, Mario Kart World’s pure casual racing isn’t all that different. If I’m sitting down to play with friends and family in a standard race, we’re getting a similar experience to what’s come before. It’s still great, and it looks fabulous on Switch 2, but it’s Mario Kart. What else is there? Pop the hood and you’ll see.

I know people were disappointed by the open world, which some saw as World’s defining feature. And in many ways, it is a little lacklustre, though that’s partially down to expectations. It’s not some Forza-esque world where you need to tick off a checklist; it’s really just a big playground with things you can do, and nothing you have to do.

I spent a lot of time just driving from place to place, checking out every corner of the map, hunting for P Switches and collectibles. But it allowed me to take in the beauty of each environment. The way the lush leaves surrounding Peach Stadium slowly melted from green to gold in Cheep Cheep Falls. I’d drop into the river and let the currents sweep me down to Salty Salty Speedway, where the rust-coloured cobbles would spit me out into the ocean, towards the desecrated ruins of Wario Shipyard.

From the rotting fortress next to the ship, I could see the icy lands to the north, the sparkly beach to the south. Never before could my eyes take me this far in Mario Kart. These weren’t just a bunch of courses plucked out of thin air; they all made sense in their order, their placement, and their layout.

The open world is your oyster, and you can do anything in it. Make up pretend races, go find that Donut Plains tribute, take really stupid photos. Where many racing games are a kaleidoscope of colours and a shock to the senses, MKW gives me a moment to breathe. Breath of the Wild did the same: there’s stuff to do in the world, and there is a story, but ultimately, the world is a canvas and I am its paintbrush.

So, when I’m on the courses, when I’m racing others or completing optional challenges, my tools are more expansive than ever. I think Mario Kart is often oversimplified, but World adds a ton of mechanics to the experience that make it the deepest kart racer I’ve ever played.

I’m good at 8 Deluxe because the racing is ultimately pretty simple, and I know the courses well. One match online in Mario Kart World is a complete bloodbath and I’m left in the dust because everyone and their mother is amazing at wall jumping and grinding and skipping swathes of the course, only for them to finish in third behind two racers who are just world-class.

I’m over the moon whenever I do a perfect wall-ride and hop in an alleyway to reach a Peach Medallion, but ask me to replicate that in an online Knockout Tour to hit 16th over 17th and I buckle under the pressure, the sweaty hands, and the barrage of red shells hitting me and slowing my speed. I can’t just pick up coins and aim perfectly and drift at just the right time to win anymore; I actually have to learn how to play the game intimately. I admire that approach.

The amount of tricks and backflips required to shave milliseconds off of a race time is absurd, but watching someone pull off all the tricks in the book successfully really is something to behold.

Really, all I do whenever I race online (or with friends) is embrace the chaos of those 24-man races. Knockout Tour, to this day, is my favourite aspect of World. It’s a complete mess, where balance and skill can and do matter, but in a Mario Party sense, you sometimes just have to succumb to fate or lack of luck.

The sheer amount of litter on the road is enough to make me both laugh and send me into a fit of panic. Why do we need this many bananas or coins or traps in one spot, let alone the fact I’m trying to avoid triple red shells around my neighbouring competitors (yes, I’m sandwiched between them).

When I am good enough to make it to the final four, it makes for an amazing 10-15 minutes of white-knuckle racing, hair-splitting differences, and scrambling to the finish line. Do I care if I win because of luck? Not really! It’s Mario Kart; I’m here to have fun, to embrace the chaos, and then decompress afterwards.

That last part is the key part that makes all the difference, the part that makes this Mario Kart cocktail taste different from the last one. Mario Kart has always been a series I’ve brought out for a few races with friends for an hour or two, and then it just gets tucked away until the next get-together. We’ll chat between races, but now we’re not just leaving it on an idle screen, we’re racing around the savannah or the black volcanic terrain, taking in the sights.

I called Mario Kart World “the perfect introduction” to the new generation of Nintendo, a year ago, and I feel even stronger about that now than I did then. In Donkey Kong Bananza, you can smash things up to your heart's content. In Pokémon Pokopia, you can rebuild the world however you wish. In Kirby Air Riders, speed and drama is unlimited. All of these games blend familiarity, calmness, chaos, and freedom. And Mario Kart World started it all.

I know it’s not a huge reinvention, but it is a shift. The Switch 2 is capable of so much more than the Switch, and World showed us that from the very first day. Do I want a bit more than 30 courses? Sure, I’d take DLC, but the year of patches have also done a lot to iron out some of the rougher edges.

Before 8 Deluxe, Mario Kart never got DLC. There are ample opportunities in the open-world to make your own fun, and once you let go of the winning mentality, Knockout Tour is a blast.

Mario Kart 8 Deluxe is Mario Kart as we’ve long known it, but World is something a bit different. It isn’t just a soda water with lime, it’s the most-refreshing summer blend of citrus flavours where you can either chill out or have chaotic fun. A defining game for the Switch 2, and worthy of much more love than it gets.

How do you feel about Mario Kart World a year after release? Let us know in the comments.



https://www.nintendolife.com/features/opinion-mario-kart-world-deserves-its-flowers-so-lets-stop-comparing-it-to-mk8-deluxe
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Psyvariar 3 Review
item.content:encoded:
If you’re just a casual consumer of arcade shoot-em-up games, you’d be surprised how deep and complicated the mechanics in these games can be. Sure, it’s easy to pick any one up, hold down the shoot button while you dodge bullets and spam Continues, then finish it in 2 hours and just kind of move on. They’re kind of the ideal game for turning your brain off and wasting a little time… if you play them like that. Lots of bullet hell games, though, bake in extra mechanics that might incentivise you to really strategise the way you play, or care about the score points you’re racking up for more than just bragging rights. Psyvariar 3 is one such game, with a core mechanic built around one of the most unique takes on bullet-grazing I’ve ever seen in a shmup.

Yes, this is Psyvariar 3, but don’t worry about the number in the title too much. We’re following Fallout 3 rules here. Besides, narrative and story are very rarely a major concern in an arcade shooter, and that really isn’t the case here. Despite opening with a lore-filled text crawl that’s so long it makes the background music loop back for a 2nd play, the meat of Psyvariar 3 is in the gameplay. Steering clear of projectiles is the goal to survival, but the Buzz mechanic encourages you to get as close as possible to the very edge of enemy bullets without being hit by them.

Do that enough and you’ll trigger a Level Up, and each time you do that a shield will temporarily envelop your ship that makes you invincible. So, your combat loop consists of constantly hovering near enemy fire, chaining together rapid Level Ups, and using the shields they provide to survive bullet swarms and get closer to enemies for the kill.

It’s thoughtful, and exhilarating, and challenging. It really reminds me of the kind of multi-mechanic ballet that you grapple with in a game like Doom Eternal. You’ve got a lot of specific actions to perform, but when you’re in the zone and Buzzing past beams and lasers it feels incredible.

There are 7 different characters to play as, and each one modifies the flow of gameplay with unique abilities and weapons. Standard starting character Naomi has a pretty basic all-rounder kit. Maeda, meanwhile, is fully Buzz-focused – he earns more points from Buzzing, and his super-powerful Bomb attack is actually just a bullet magnet that reverses bullets back at the enemies, letting you trigger more Buzz on them as they whizz by you. Then there’s V.M.O, who has a shield-focused kit that keeps you focused on constantly chaining together enough Level Ups to keep said shield activated.

As fascinating and inventive as this mechanic is, one major gripe I have is that the game does absolutely nothing to tutorialize you on it or introduce it to you. Now, this is something that a lot of older and mechanically dense arcade shooters do. No tutorial, just tossing you in and expecting you to learn from practice and word-of-mouth. For a proper console release in the big 2026, though, it feels a little absurd that the most the game does to tutorialize you is with a very brief mechanic-explainer pop-up window you only see if you happen to press a specific button at the character select screen. Sure, there’s also a character-specific Mission mode that’s tailored to mastering each pilot’s techniques. That ends up feeling more like a fun score-chase series than proper onboarding, though.

If you’re in the mood for a simpler shoot-and-survive kind of vibe, there’s also a really fun Arrange mode available in the game. In this version of the campaign, there are way more bullets, but you’re also at max power the entire time. No need to worry about Buzzing and Level Ups, just shoot and dodge and die a lot. I don’t always understand what the difference is between Arrange mode and standard mode in some shooters, but the mechanically dense nature of Psyvariar 3s main mode made me especially appreciate the differences and changes they put into the Arrange mode.

Overall, Psyvariar 3 is a really fun and really unique arcade shooter experience. It has mechanics that are tough to learn, but incredibly fun to master. While the presentation and UI design can be a little basic at times, and it’s absolutely lacking in proper tutorials, I still had a great time Buzzing my way through every level.



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/06/07/psyvariar-3-review/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Xbox Games Showcase 2026 live report - all the news and announcements as Asha Sharma presents for the first time
item.content:encoded:
The Xbox Games Showcase is today, Sunday 7th June, at 6pm BST (7pm CEST / 1pm EDT / 10am PDT), and we're covering all the news and announcements from live, right here, in this article.

Updates appear automatically so you don't need to refresh the page and our coverage-proper begins closer to the show at around 5.30pm. But we'll be waffling in the lead-up to it. Be sure to leave a comment and join in - you know the drill.

What do we expect from today's Xbox show? The only thing we know for sure is the Gears of War prequel E-Day will have a dedicated deep-dive segment after the main showcase, which is something Microsoft has done for other games in the recent past.

We also know from an Xbox Podcast (via GameSpot) that Microsoft's next-gen Xbox console Project Helix definitely won't feature - boo hiss! "The focus here is on the games," Microsoft Studios boss Matt Booty said. "There won’t be Helix news. Helix will not be in this showcase. We want to get everything about that right."

Nevertheless, today's show is a big moment for new Xbox leader Asha Sharma, who's spent months telling everyone she's going to "reset" the Xbox division and make the console a more aggressive contender again. Today's her chance to solidify that message and convince a core gaming crowd. But how will she do that?

Games, naturally, will be at the heart of it, and to that end I think we're likely to see more of inXile's promising time-beding RPG Clockwork Revolution, and maybe get a release date for it. We're probably going to see more of zombie survival game State of Decay 3 - and maybe some actual gameplay this time - and there's a rumour going around that Toys for Bob is rebooting Spyro, so might we see that here as well?

What of the big-guns, though? I wonder if it's time for Bethesda to wheel out The Elder Scrolls 6 cannon and end the showcase with an almighty bang - it's been long enough since Starfield now that it makes sense. And is it also time for a glimpse at the Blade game Arkane is working on? There's plenty of potential excitement in the wings.

Join us later at just before 6pm for the full show.



https://www.eurogamer.net/xbox-games-showcase-2026-live-report

item.title: Star Trek: Shadow Frontier announced by Bloober Team
item.content:encoded:
I bet this was not on anyone’s (not) E3 Bingo cards, horror masters and Layers of Fear creators. Bloober Team, are making a Star Trek game!

Star Trek: Shadow Frontier will star fan favourite Ro Laren who has crashed on what appears to be a sentient planet. The trailer looks suitably spooky with slithering tentacles, scuttling creatures, and a voice-over intoning “this time there is no coming back from this”

Ro is a Bajoran who served on the Enterprise D under Jean-Luc Picard, before defecting to the Maquis. After serving time in prison, she returned to Starfleet and ended up on Deep Space Nine as head of security. She was last seen in the TV series Picard where she met her demise.

The actress who played Ro in the television shows, Michelle Forbes, has returned for voice and motion capture for the game.

As for the voice heard in the trailer, it does seem that Ro knows who it is, so it could be an established character.

“As she explores the planet’s corrupted surface and crosses paths with other survivors, she must face twisted creatures, a hostile ecosystem, and an entity that seeks to envelop her body and mind,” states the press release.

The game is ‘coming soon’ on PC via Steam, PS5, Xbox Series X|S, and Nintendo Switch 2.

Source: Press Release / YouTube



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/06/07/star-trek-shadow-frontier-announced-by-bloober-team/

item.title: Asha Sharma’s New Big Xbox Call – Yank Gears of War: E-Day Off PS5 and Rebuild the Console Exclusivity Wall
item.content:encoded:
• 0-20%: Unlikely - Lacks credible sources
  • 21-40%: Questionable - Some concerns remain
  • 41-60%: Plausible - Reasonable evidence
  • 61-80%: Probable - Strong evidence
  • 81-100%: Highly Likely - Multiple reliable sources

Just a few hours ahead of the Xbox Games Showcase 2026, insider Jeff Grubb revealed during a Giant Bomb stream that The Coalition's Gears of War: E-Day was supposed to launch on PlayStation 5 but won't anymore.

I'll say it here. Gears of War: E-Day was going to be on PS5; it's not anymore. That just happened. Like, it's a decision that just got made. And it's like, they're going backwards on this stuff where they were like, this battle doesn't make any sense.

Indeed, Microsoft had chosen to go fully multiplatform with many of its games. Gears of War: Reloaded, for instance, brought the series to PlayStation consoles for the first time. It seemed like the obvious opening to E-Day, the next chapter in the series (although technically a prequel), also being released on PS5. Officially, the game was announced only for PC and Xbox Series S and X, but a PS5 version seemed almost inevitable.

Everything changed in the last few months, though, with Asha Sharma taking over from Phil Spencer as Chief Executive Officer of Xbox. She is trying to re-establish the core Xbox console business, for example, which has led her to rethink the previous multiplatform mandate. In a recent Bloomberg Tech interview, she outlined that, as the second-largest gaming publisher, Xbox must reach large audiences, but as a platform, exclusive content is important, too.

Gears of War: E-Day walking back the (unannounced) PS5 version could be a sign of other such changes. For example, Fable is currently scheduled to launch next February with a day-one PS5 version; whether that still holds with this strategy shift remains to be seen.

Anyway, we'll know a lot more about Gears of War: E-Day later today, as the game is getting a dedicated show after the general Xbox showcase. Stay tuned.

About the author: With over two decades of experience in gaming journalism, Alessio Palumbo has led the gaming vertical at Wccftech since August 2015. He started working at a young age for Italian websites like Everyeye.it, Gamestar.it, Nextgame.it, and Multiplayer.it before kickstarting the indie English-language publication Worlds Factory as its founder and Editor in Chief. In the last decade, he has coordinated the overall output of Wccftech's gaming section, managed PR relations, assigned reviews, produced daily news coverage, edited gaming content as needed, and delivered game reviews. Arguably, his trademark content is the long series of exclusive developer interviews that have been cited by Wikipedia and by the biggest news media and gaming publications. His passion for technology also makes him knowledgeable when it comes to gaming hardware and tech. His favorite genres include RPGs, MMORPGs, and action/adventure games.

item.title: Poll: Box Art Brawl - Duel: Rayman 3D
item.content:encoded:
Ray Tracing

Walk this Ray, it's time for another edition of Box Art Brawl.

Last week, we matched up three covers for the GameCube's excellent WarioWare, Inc: Mega Party Game$!, and you lot were clearly on the same page as us, crowning the North American design the best of the bunch with 76% of the vote. Poor old Europe and Japan had to split the remainder with 12% apiece.

With all of this week's Rayman news, we're taking a look at the limbless legend's last 3D outing in the aptly-titled Rayman 3D. This port of the Dreamcast's Rayman 2 may not have blown our socks off when it arrived on 3DS back in 2011, but the 3D visuals looked great on the handheld screen, and it was a nice excuse to get all nostalgic for the extra-dimension platforming of yore.

There are just two covers to choose between this week, so let's dive into a good old battle of NA vs EU.

North America

While Mr. Ray himself might look a little uncanny in the North American cover, there's no denying that this homage to the Rayman 2 original art has some cool composition at play. The hair overlapping the title? Cool. The splintering walkway? Cool. The giant exploding ship in the background? Cool. Overall, pretty cool.

Europe

The European design is... not as cool. Rayman himself looks much better, but all of the action and drama of the NA cover has been replaced by simplicity. We've got a plain blue backdrop and a singular Lum. That's it. Hey, at least the title font is a little cleaner.

Thank you for voting! We'll see you next week for another edition of Box Art Brawl!

Jim came to Nintendo Life in 2022 and, despite his insistence that The Minish Cap is the best Zelda game and his unwavering love for the Star Wars prequels (yes, really), he has continued to write news and features on the site ever since.

  • Author Profile
  • Bluesky
  • Reply

item.title: Cook And Sell Codes (June 2026)
item.content:encoded:
Use these Cook And Sell monthly redeem codes to get your hands on plenty of free rewards, including Cash, and other cosmetics.

Cook And Sell is a Roblox cooking and business simulation game is a fast-paced kitchen environment where speed, efficiency, and customer satisfaction determine success. Players must prepare meals, serve hungry customers, and strategically manage their growing food business while earning profits and unlocking new recipes and upgrades.

Success in Cook And Sell comes from mastering the complete business cycle: buying ingredients from your desk, unboxing them in the kitchen, combining them in your cooking pot, and displaying finished products to attract buyers. Players must scan customer purchases and process payments while maintaining inventory levels.

Currently in its alpha development phase, Cook And Sell has been building momentum since its launch but it has attracted quite a few players. So much so, that it already has over 19 million visits with over 180k favourites as of June 2026.

Active Cook And Sell Codes

  • BONUS – 200 cash
  • YUM – 150 cash

Expired Cook And Sell Codes

As of June 2026, there are no expired Cook and Sell codes

How To Redeem Cook And Sell Codes

Redeem codes in Cook And Sell is relatively simple, like the majority of Roblox games. Here’s how players can easily redeem any and all codes in Cook And Sell:

  1. Boot Cook And Sell on Roblox
  2. Click on the codes button on the left hand side of the screen
  3. Type or paste the code into the ‘enter code’ box
  4. Hit ‘claim’ to get your freebie!

Where To Get More Cook And Sell Codes

Cook And Sell codes don’t just materialize out of thin air—you need to know where the developers actually share them. Your primary hub should be the official Cook And Sell Discord server, where the development team drops fresh codes whenever there’s a content update, event launch, or player milestone celebration. These usually get announced in dedicated channels, so joining and enabling notifications is your smartest move.

Beyond Discord, follow the game’s official Roblox group page. Developers occasionally post codes there during special occasions or when the game hits follower benchmarks. It’s not as frequent as Discord, but it’s still worth checking. The creator’s Twitter or YouTube channel might also feature codes during promotional videos or community shout-outs, especially when they’re trying to boost engagement around a new feature.

Codes in Cook And Sell tend to drop around updates, seasonal events, or bug patch rollouts—but there’s zero in-game notification system. Thus, the easiest strategy is to save this guide in your browser and check back instead of trying to monitor every source yourself.

This page is refreshed as soon as fresh codes go live, and any codes that have expired are taken out quickly so you always see only the ones that still work.

item.title: New PowerWash Simulator 2: Star Wars Pack Trailer Spotlights Classic Locations | IGN Live 2026
item.content:encoded:
At IGN Live Saturday, attendees got a first look at a new trailer for the PowerWash Simulator 2: Star Wars Pack DLC. When George Lucas created Star Wars, one of the things that stood out about it compared to other sci-fi movies of its era was the lived in aesthetic, which included the kind of dirt and grime rarely seen up until that point in a genre that usually went for clean and pristine. But as much as things can be dirty in Star Wars, sometimes you gotta take a look at your filthy Gonk droid and realize it's time to wash that sucker!

In this Star Wars take on PowerWash Simulator 2, players will play as a class five labor droid, P0-W2. The new trailer provides more glimpses of the different iconic locations and vehicles featured, all pulled from the original trilogy era that the PowerWash Simulator 2: Star Wars Pack is set in, including the Lars Homestead, Chalmun's Cantina, X-Wing fighters, AT-ATs, and the bridge of a Super Star Destroyer... And trust me, you are gonna want to make that bridge look good before Lord Vader comes back and starts Force choking you.

The Star Wars Pack for PowerWash Simulator 2 will be available on PlayStation 5 (PS5), PlayStation 5 Pro (PS5 Pro), PC, Xbox Series X/S, and Nintendo Switch 2 on July 16, 2026.

Be sure to keep up with everything going on at IGN Live 2026. You can check the full schedule to see what else is coming up across games, movies, TV, streaming, and more.

Nicholas Galitzine ("Adam / He-Man"), Camila Mendes ("Teela") and Idris Elba ("Man-at-Arms") got their own first look at the game and how their respective characters appear in it.
